title.zh: 卷七十四 魏相丙吉傳
title.en: Volume 74: Wei Xiang and Bing Ji
title.raw: 卷七十四 魏相丙吉傳 Volume 74: Wei Xiang and Bing Ji

sentences.zh: 魏相字弱翁，濟陰定陶人也，徙平陵。

translations.literal: Wei Xiang, courtesy name Ruoweng, was a man of Dingtao in Jiyin; he moved to Pingling.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang, courtesy name Ruoweng, came from Dingtao in Jiyin and later relocated his household to Pingling.

sentences.zh: 少學《易》，為郡卒史，舉賢良，以對策高第，為茂陵令。

translations.literal: In youth he studied the Changes, became a commandery clerk, was recommended as worthy and good, by policy answers in high rank became magistrate of Maoling.
translations.idiomatic: He studied the Book of Changes in youth, served as a commandery clerk, was recommended as worthy and good, ranked high on the policy examination, and was appointed magistrate of Maoling.

sentences.zh: 頃之，御史大夫桑弘羊客詐稱御史止傳，丞不以時謁，客怒縛丞。

translations.literal: Before long, a retainer of Imperial Clerk Sang Hongyang falsely claimed to be an imperial clerk and stopped at the relay station; the assistant magistrate did not in due time pay respects, and the retainer in anger bound the assistant.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward a client of Imperial Clerk Sang Hongyang posed as an imperial clerk at a post station; when the assistant magistrate failed to appear promptly, the man flew into a rage and had him tied up.

sentences.zh: 相疑其有奸，收捕，案致其罪，論棄客市，茂陵大治。

translations.literal: Xiang suspected he had villainy, seized and arrested him, investigated and brought his crime, sentenced and exposed the retainer in the market—Maoling was greatly well governed.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang suspected foul play, made the arrest, tried the case, had the impostor executed in the marketplace, and brought Maoling to good order.

sentences.zh: 後遷河南太守，禁止奸邪，豪強畏服。

translations.literal: Later he was transferred to governor of Henan, forbidding wickedness; the powerful and arrogant feared and submitted.
translations.idiomatic: Promoted to governor of Henan, he suppressed wrongdoing until even the great families submitted.

sentences.zh: 會丞相車千秋死，先是千秋子為雒陽武庫令，自見失父，而相治郡嚴，恐久獲罪，乃自免去。

translations.literal: It happened that Chancellor Che Qianqiu died; earlier Qianqiu’s son had been Luoyang armory director. Seeing himself bereft of his father, while Xiang governed the commandery strictly, fearing that in time he would incur guilt, he resigned of his own accord.
translations.idiomatic: When Chancellor Che Qianqiu died, his son—who had been director of the Luoyang armory—feared that under Wei Xiang’s strict rule he might eventually be charged with some fault, and resigned without waiting to be dismissed.

sentences.zh: 相使掾追呼之，遂不肯還。

translations.literal: Xiang sent a clerk to pursue and call him back; he then would not return.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang sent a clerk after him to bring him back, but the man refused to return.

sentences.zh: 相獨恨曰：「大將軍聞此令去官，必以為我用丞相死不能遇其子。

translations.literal: Xiang alone regretted, saying: ‘If the Grand General hears of this order to leave office, he will certainly think that because I used the chancellor’s death I could not accommodate his son.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang brooded alone: ‘If Grand General Huo Guang learns that I let the chancellor’s son quit, he will assume I used the old chancellor’s death to hound the heir out of office.

sentences.zh: 使當世貴人非我，殆矣！」

translations.literal: If the eminent men of the age turn against me, I am in peril!’
translations.idiomatic: If the great men at court turn against me, I am finished.’

sentences.zh: 武庫令西至長安，大將軍霍光果以責過相曰：「幼主新立，以為函谷京師之固，武庫精兵所聚，故以丞相弟為關都尉，子為武庫令。

translations.literal: When the armory director went west to Chang’an, Grand General Huo Guang indeed blamed Xiang, saying: ‘The young lord is newly established; we thought Hangu Pass is the bulwark of the capital, the armory where picked troops are massed—therefore we made the chancellor’s younger brother pass commandant and his son armory director.
translations.idiomatic: When the armory director reached Chang’an, Huo Guang rebuked Wei Xiang: ‘The boy emperor has just ascended the throne; we posted the chancellor’s brother at Hangu Pass and his son at the armory because the pass guards the capital and the depot holds the crack troops.

sentences.zh: 今河南太守不深惟國家大策，苟見丞相不在而斥逐其子，何淺薄也！」

translations.literal: Now the governor of Henan does not deeply ponder the state’s great strategy; he merely sees the chancellor absent and drives off his son—how shallow!’
translations.idiomatic: Your governor never weighed the larger design; he saw only that the chancellor was gone and drove the son away—how petty!’

sentences.zh: 後人有告相賊殺不辜，事下有司。

translations.literal: Afterward someone reported that Xiang had murdered the innocent; the matter was sent down to the responsible offices.
translations.idiomatic: Later an informer accused Wei Xiang of killing innocents, and the case was referred to the judiciary.

sentences.zh: 河南卒戍中都官者二三千人，遮大將軍，自言願復留作一年以贖太守罪。

translations.literal: Henan men garrisoned in the capital offices, two or three thousand, blocked the Grand General, declaring themselves willing to serve another year to redeem the governor’s crime.
translations.idiomatic: Two or three thousand Henan conscripts then serving in the capital barracks waylaid Huo Guang and begged to work an extra year of labor to atone for their governor.

sentences.zh: 河南老弱萬餘人守關欲入上書，關吏以聞。

translations.literal: More than ten thousand old and weak of Henan guarded the pass wishing to enter and submit memorials; the pass clerks reported it.
translations.idiomatic: Over ten thousand Henan elders and commoners massed at the barrier, demanding to enter the capital with petitions; the gate officers reported the uproar.

sentences.zh: 大將軍用武庫令事，遂下相廷尉獄。

translations.literal: The Grand General used the armory director affair, then sent Xiang down to the commandant of justice’s prison.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang seized on the armory incident and had Wei Xiang thrown into the commandant of justice’s jail.

sentences.zh: 久系逾冬，會赦出。

translations.literal: Long detained past winter, it happened that an amnesty released him.
translations.idiomatic: He remained in chains through the winter until a general amnesty freed him.

sentences.zh: 復有詔守茂陵令，遷楊州刺史。

translations.literal: Again there was an edict to guard as Maoling magistrate; he was promoted Yangzhou inspector.
translations.idiomatic: An edict restored him as magistrate of Maoling, then promoted him to inspector of Yangzhou.

sentences.zh: 考案郡國守相，多所貶退。

translations.literal: Investigating commandery and kingdom governors and chancellors, many were demoted and dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: In his inspections of regional governors and chancellors he demoted or removed many from office.

sentences.zh: 相與丙吉相善，時吉為光祿大夫，與相書曰：「朝廷已深知弱翁治行，方且大用矣。

translations.literal: Xiang was on good terms with Bing Ji; at the time Ji was supernumerary household grandee and wrote to Xiang: ‘The court already deeply knows Ruoweng’s administrative conduct; he is about to be greatly employed.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang was close to Bing Ji, then a supernumerary household grandee, who wrote: ‘The court knows your record well and is poised to give you higher office.

sentences.zh: 願少慎事自重，臧器於身。」

translations.literal: I wish you would slightly be careful in affairs and value yourself, storing the vessel in your person.’
translations.idiomatic: Take care in small matters and husband your strength—keep your talents in reserve.’

sentences.zh: 相心善其言，為霽威嚴。

translations.literal: Xiang thought well of his words and relaxed his severity.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang took the advice to heart and softened his harsh manner.

sentences.zh: 居部二歲，徵為諫大夫，復為河南太守。

translations.literal: After two years in his department, he was summoned as remonstrance grandee, again governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: After two years in that post he was recalled as remonstrance grandee, then again made governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 元康中，匈奴遣兵擊漢屯田車師者，不能下。

translations.literal: In the Yuankang period, the Xiongnu sent troops to attack Han’s military colonists at Cheshi but could not take them.
translations.idiomatic: During Yuankang the Xiongnu attacked Han’s garrison colonists at Cheshi but failed to overrun them.

sentences.zh: 上與後將軍趙充國等議，欲因匈奴衰弱，出兵擊其右地，使不敢復擾西域。

translations.literal: The sovereign deliberated with Rear General Zhao Chongguo and others, wishing to exploit the Xiongnu’s weakness, send troops to strike their right territory, and make them not dare again to disturb the Western Regions.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor met with Rear General Zhao Chongguo and others, planning to exploit Xiongnu weakness with a strike into their western flank so they would never again trouble the Western Regions.

sentences.zh: 相上書諫曰：臣聞之，救亂誅暴，謂之義兵，兵義者王；

translations.literal: Xiang submitted a memorial remonstrating: Your servant has heard that rescuing chaos and punishing violence is called the righteous army; he who wields righteous arms is king;
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang remonstrated: ‘I have read that armies raised to rescue the people and punish cruelty are righteous hosts, and righteous hosts win the mandate.

sentences.zh: 敵加於己，不得已而起者，謂之應兵，兵應者勝；

translations.literal: When the enemy presses on oneself and one rises because there is no choice, it is called the responsive army; he who wields responsive arms prevails;
translations.idiomatic: Troops raised because invasion leaves no choice are responsive hosts, and responsive hosts win their wars.

sentences.zh: 爭恨小故，不忍憤怒者，謂之忿兵，兵忿者敗；

translations.literal: Quarreling over petty causes, unable to bear anger, is called the angry army; he who wields angry arms is defeated;
translations.idiomatic: Armies stirred by private pique are angry hosts, and angry hosts lose.

sentences.zh: 利人土地貨寶者，謂之貪兵，兵貪者破；

translations.literal: Coveting others’ land, goods, and treasure is called the greedy army; he who wields greedy arms is broken;
translations.idiomatic: Armies mobilized for plunder are greedy hosts, and greedy hosts are ruined.

sentences.zh: 恃國家之大，矜民人之眾，欲見威於敵者，謂之驕兵，兵驕者滅：此五者，非但人事，乃天道也。

translations.literal: Relying on the state’s greatness, boasting of the multitude of the people, wishing to display might to the enemy is called the arrogant army; he who wields arrogant arms is destroyed: these five are not only human affairs—they are Heaven’s Way too.
translations.idiomatic: Hosts swollen with national pride are arrogant hosts, and arrogant hosts perish. These five outcomes are not mere human opinion—they follow Heaven’s own pattern.

sentences.zh: 間者匈奴嘗有善意，所得漢民輒奉歸之，未有犯於邊境，雖爭屯田車師，不足致意中。

translations.literal: Recently the Xiongnu have shown good intentions; whenever they took Han people they returned them; they have not violated the border; though they contest the Cheshi colonies, it is not worth fixing attention on.
translations.idiomatic: Lately the Xiongnu have returned captured Han subjects and kept the frontier quiet; their scuffle over Cheshi hardly merits the court’s obsession.

sentences.zh: 今聞諸將軍欲興兵入其地，臣愚不知此兵何名者也。

translations.literal: Now I hear the generals wish to raise troops and enter their land; your servant foolishly does not know what name this army would bear.
translations.idiomatic: Yet now the generals want to march into their territory; I cannot see which of the five kinds of host such a campaign would be.

sentences.zh: 今邊郡困乏，父子共犬羊之裘，食草萊之實，常恐不能自存，難以動兵。

translations.literal: Now the border commanderies are destitute; fathers and sons share dog-and-sheep pelts, eat wild greens and seeds, constantly fear they cannot preserve themselves—it is hard to move armies.
translations.idiomatic: The border counties are exhausted: families share sheepskin wraps and forage weeds, always fearing they cannot survive another season—hardly a moment to stir new armies.

sentences.zh: 『軍旅之後，必有凶年』，言民以其愁苦之氣，傷陰陽之和也。

translations.literal: ‘After armies and hosts, there must be a bad year’—this means the people, with their bitter grieving qi, harm the harmony of yin and yang.
translations.idiomatic: The classic warns that after every great campaign comes famine, because the people’s grief throws yin and yang out of balance.

sentences.zh: 出兵雖勝，猶有後憂，恐災害之變因此以生。

translations.literal: Even if troops go out and win, still there is later worry; I fear changes of disaster and calamity may thereby arise.
translations.idiomatic: Even a victorious expedition leaves trouble behind, and I dread the omens that may follow.

sentences.zh: 今郡國守、相多不實選，風俗尤薄，水旱不時。

translations.literal: Now commandery and kingdom governors and chancellors are mostly not truly selected; customs are especially thin; flood and drought do not come at their seasons.
translations.idiomatic: Governors and chancellors are too often unworthy appointees, popular morals are thin, and rain and drought ignore the calendar.

sentences.zh: 案今年計，子弟殺父兄、妻殺夫者，凡二百二十二人，臣愚以為此非小變也。

translations.literal: According to this year’s registers, sons and younger brothers who killed fathers or elder brothers, and wives who killed husbands, total two hundred twenty-two persons; your servant foolishly considers this no small change.
translations.idiomatic: This year’s returns list 222 cases of sons or brothers murdering fathers or elders and wives murdering husbands—no minor sign of decay.

sentences.zh: 今左右不憂此，乃欲發兵報纖介之忿於遠夷，殆孔子所謂『吾恐季孫之憂不在顓臾而在蕭牆之內』也。

translations.literal: Now those at your side do not worry over this, yet wish to raise armies to repay a hair’s-breadth grudge on distant barbarians—this is probably what Confucius meant by ‘I fear the Ji family’s trouble is not with Zhuanyu but within the screen wall.’
translations.idiomatic: Those about you ignore this rot yet talk of war over a trifling score with distant tribes—exactly Confucius’s fear that the Ji clan’s peril lay not in Zhuanyu but inside its own walls.

sentences.zh: 願陛下與平昌侯、樂昌侯、平恩侯及有識者詳議乃可。」

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty with the Marquises of Pingchang and Lechang, the Marquis of Ping’en, and men of insight to deliberate in detail—only then may it be done.’
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to take counsel with the Marquises of Pingchang, Lechang, and Ping’en and with every wise minister before you act.’

sentences.zh: 上從相言而止。

translations.literal: The sovereign followed Xiang’s words and desisted.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted Wei Xiang’s advice and dropped the campaign.

sentences.zh: 相明《易經》，有師法，好觀漢故事及便宜章奏，以為古今異制，方今務在奉行故事而已。

translations.literal: Xiang was clear on the Changes classic, had a teacher’s transmission, liked to read Han precedents and convenient memorials, and held that ancient and modern differ in institutions; the present task lies in carrying out precedents.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang mastered the Zhouyi under a recognized master, loved Han precedents and practical memorials, and held that one must not copy antiquity wholesale—the urgent need was to enforce what had already proven sound.

sentences.zh: 數條漢興已來國家便宜行事，及賢臣賈誼、朝錯、董仲舒等所言，奏請施行之，曰：「臣聞明主在上，賢輔在下，則君安虞而民和睦。

translations.literal: He repeatedly itemized since Han rose the state’s convenient practices and what worthy ministers Jia Yi, Chao Cuo, Dong Zhongshu, and others said, memorializing to request their implementation, saying: ‘Your servant has heard that when a clear master is above and worthy assistants below, the ruler is secure and the people harmonious.
translations.idiomatic: He listed policy successes since Han’s founding and memorials by Jia Yi, Chao Cuo, Dong Zhongshu, and others, asking that they be put into practice, and wrote: ‘When a wise sovereign sits the throne and able ministers serve below, the ruler rests easy and the people live in harmony.

sentences.zh: 臣相幸得備位，不能奉明法，廣教化，理四方，以宣聖德。

translations.literal: Your servant Xiang has fortunately attained a place but cannot uphold clear law, broaden transformation through instruction, or put the four quarters in order to spread sagely virtue.
translations.idiomatic: I have undeservedly reached high office yet fail to clarify the laws, spread moral instruction, or pacify the four quarters in the sage-kings’ fashion.

sentences.zh: 民多背本趨末，或有饑寒之色，為陛下之憂，臣相罪當萬死。

translations.literal: The people mostly turn their backs on root and rush to branch trades; some have the look of cold and hunger—this is worry for Your Majesty; your servant Xiang’s crime deserves myriad deaths.
translations.idiomatic: The people flock to petty trades and show the pallor of hunger—Your Majesty’s worry, and my fault deserving death ten thousand times.

sentences.zh: 臣相知能淺薄，不明國家大體，明用之宜，惟民終始，未得所由。

translations.literal: Your servant Xiang’s knowledge and ability are shallow and thin; he does not understand the great frame of the state or the fitness of enlightened employment, nor the people’s beginning and end—he has not grasped the source.
translations.idiomatic: I am shallow and ignorant of the state’s larger design, of how to employ men wisely, or of what governs the people’s welfare from start to finish.

sentences.zh: 竊伏觀先帝聖德仁恩之厚，勤勞天下，垂意黎庶，憂水旱之災，為民貧窮發倉廩，賑乏餧；

translations.literal: Your servant humbly has observed the Former Emperor’s sagely virtue and kind grace in their thickness, his toil for all under Heaven, his care for the black-haired masses, his worry over flood and drought disaster, opening granaries for the people’s poverty, relieving the exhausted and starving;
translations.idiomatic: I have studied the late emperor’s kindness: he labored for the realm, cared for the commoners, feared flood and drought, opened granaries for the destitute, and fed the starving.

sentences.zh: 遣諫大夫博士巡行天下，察風俗，舉賢良，平冤獄，冠蓋交道；

translations.literal: He dispatched remonstrance grandees and erudites to tour the empire, observe customs, recommend worthy and good, set right unjust prisons—caps and canopies crossed on the roads;
translations.idiomatic: He sent remonstrance grandees and erudites to tour the empire, survey customs, recommend talent, and redress wrongful convictions until official carriages jammed the highways.

sentences.zh: 省諸用，寬租賦，弛山澤波池，禁秣馬酤酒貯積，所以周急繼困，慰安元元，便利百姓之道甚備。

translations.literal: He reduced various expenditures, relaxed rent and taxes, opened mountains, marshes, pools, and ponds, forbade feeding horses grain, retailing wine, and hoarding grain—the means to relieve urgency and succor distress, comfort the common folk, and benefit the hundred surnames were very complete.
translations.idiomatic: He cut spending, lowered taxes, opened reserved hills and waters, banned grain-fed horses, wine peddling, and hoarding—every device to aid the desperate and steady the people was tried.

sentences.zh: 臣相不能悉陳，昧死奏故事詔書凡二十三事。

translations.literal: Your servant Xiang cannot set them forth entire; risking death he memorializes precedents and edicts totaling twenty-three items.
translations.idiomatic: I cannot list them all; at peril of my life I submit twenty-three precedents and edicts for your review.

sentences.zh: 臣謹案王法必本於農而務積聚，量入制用以備凶災，亡六年之畜，尚謂之急。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully cases the kingly law: it must root in agriculture and stress accumulation, measuring income to fix expenditure to prepare for evil disaster; lacking six years’ stores is still called urgent.
translations.idiomatic: The kingly way roots in farming and stored surplus: match outlays to income and stockpile against famine; even the classic ‘six years’ reserve’ marks only a minimum.

sentences.zh: 元鼎三年，平原、勃海、太山、東郡溥被災害，民餓死於道路。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yuanding, Pingyuan, Bohai, Taishan, and Dong commandery were broadly struck by disaster; the people starved to death on the roads.
translations.idiomatic: In 116 BCE flood and drought swept Pingyuan, Bohai, Taishan, and Dong until corpses lined the roads.

sentences.zh: 二千石不豫慮其難，使至於此，賴明詔振救，乃得蒙更生。

translations.literal: The two-thousand-piculs did not beforehand plan for their hardship, letting it reach this point; relying on a clear edict to relieve and save, they then received renewed life.
translations.idiomatic: Local governors failed to plan ahead until disaster struck; only imperial relief gave the people another chance at life.

sentences.zh: 今歲不登，谷暴騰踴，臨秋收斂猶有乏者，至春恐甚，亡以相恤。

translations.literal: Now the year’s harvest has not ripened; grain has suddenly leaped in price; approaching autumn collection there are still those who lack; by spring I fear it will be worse, with nothing to succor them.
translations.idiomatic: This year’s crop is poor, grain prices have spiked, and even at harvest some still go short; by spring the distress may be worse with no relief in sight.

sentences.zh: 西羌未平，師旅在外，兵革相乘，臣竊寒心，宜早圖其備。

translations.literal: The Western Qiang are not yet pacified; armies are abroad; weapons and armor multiply one upon another—your servant secretly feels chill in the heart; it is fitting early to plan preparation.
translations.idiomatic: The western Qiang remain restless, armies are still in the field, and the people stagger under levies—I urge you to prepare now.

sentences.zh: 唯陛下留神元元，帥繇先帝盛德以撫海內。」

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty fix attention on the common folk and lead them by the Former Emperor’s great virtue to soothe all within the seas.’
translations.idiomatic: Fix your mind on the common people and guide them with the late emperor’s example so the realm may know peace.’

sentences.zh: 上施行其策。

translations.literal: The sovereign implemented his plans.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor adopted his program.

sentences.zh: 又數表采《易陰陽》及《明堂月令》奏之，曰：

translations.literal: Again he repeatedly memorialized drawing from the Changes’ yin-yang and the Ming Hall month ordinance, saying:
translations.idiomatic: He also drew repeatedly on the Changes’ yin-yang lore and the Bright Hall monthly ordinances in memorials such as this:

sentences.zh: 臣相幸得備員，奉職不修，不能宣廣教化。

translations.literal: Your servant Xiang has fortunately filled a place; in performing duty he has not cultivated it; he cannot spread and broaden transformation through instruction.
translations.idiomatic: I fill an office I have not truly earned and have failed to extend your transforming influence.

sentences.zh: 陰陽未和，災害未息，咎在臣等。

translations.literal: Yin and yang are not yet harmonious; disasters and prodigies have not ceased; the fault lies with your servants and others.
translations.idiomatic: Yin and yang remain out of tune and omens continue—the fault is mine and my colleagues’.

sentences.zh: 臣聞《易》曰：「天地以順動，故日月不過，四時不忒；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard the Changes says: ‘Heaven and earth move in accord, therefore sun and moon do not err and the four seasons are not wrong;
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes says: ‘When Heaven and earth move in harmony, sun and moon keep their courses and the seasons never falter.

sentences.zh: 聖王以順動，故刑罰清而民服。」

translations.literal: the sage king moves in accord, therefore punishments are clear and the people submit.’
translations.idiomatic: When the sage-king moves in harmony, punishments are just and the people obey.’

sentences.zh: 天地變化，必繇陰陽，陰陽之分，以日為紀。

translations.literal: Heaven and earth’s transformations must follow yin and yang; yin and yang’s divisions take the sun as their reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven and earth turn on yin and yang, and yin and yang are measured by the sun’s journey.

sentences.zh: 日冬夏至，則八風之序立，萬物之性成，各有常職，不得相干。

translations.literal: When the sun reaches winter and summer solstices, then the order of the eight winds is established, the natures of the myriad things are completed, each has its constant duty, and none may encroach on another.
translations.idiomatic: At the solstices the eight winds fall into order, every creature finds its nature, and each season keeps its office without trespass.

sentences.zh: 東方之神太昊，乘『震』執規司春；

translations.literal: The god of the east, Taihao, rides Zhen, holds the compass, and oversees spring;
translations.idiomatic: Taihao, god of the east, rides the Zhen trigram, holds the jade compass, and rules spring.

sentences.zh: 南方之神炎帝，乘『離』執衡司夏；

translations.literal: the god of the south, Yan Di, rides Li, holds the leveling beam, and oversees summer;
translations.idiomatic: Yan Di of the south rides Li, holds the leveling cord, and rules summer.

sentences.zh: 西方之神少昊，乘『兌』，執矩司秋；

translations.literal: the god of the west, Shao Hao, rides Dui, holds the square, and oversees autumn;
translations.idiomatic: Shao Hao of the west rides Dui, holds the square, and rules autumn.

sentences.zh: 北方之神顓頊，乘『坎』執權司冬；

translations.literal: the god of the north, Zhuanxu, rides Kan, holds the weight, and oversees winter;
translations.idiomatic: Zhuanxu of the north rides Kan, holds the weight, and rules winter.

sentences.zh: 中央之神黃帝，乘『坤』、『艮』執繩司下土。

translations.literal: the god of the center, the Yellow Thearch, rides Kun and Gen, holds the cord, and oversees the lower earth.
translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Thearch at the center rides Kun and Gen, holds the plumb cord, and rules the central plain.

sentences.zh: 茲五帝所司，各有時也。

translations.literal: These are what the Five Thearchs oversee; each has its season.
translations.idiomatic: Each of the Five Thearchs governs his season.

sentences.zh: 東方之卦不可以治西方，南方之卦不可以治北方。

translations.literal: The eastern trigram may not govern the west; the southern trigram may not govern the north.
translations.idiomatic: Eastern signs must not rule western affairs, nor southern ones northern.

sentences.zh: 春興『兌』治則饑，秋興『震』治則華，冬興『離』治則洩，夏興『坎』治則雹。

translations.literal: If in spring one raises Dui to govern, there is famine; if in autumn one raises Zhen to govern, there is blooming; if in winter one raises Li to govern, there is drainage; if in summer one raises Kan to govern, there is hail.
translations.idiomatic: Invoke the wrong trigram and spring Dui brings famine, autumn Zhen brings untimely bloom, winter Li brings drought, summer Kan brings hail.

sentences.zh: 明王謹於尊天，慎於養人，故立羲和之官以乘四時，節授民事。

translations.literal: The clear king is careful in honoring Heaven and cautious in nurturing the people; therefore he establishes Xi and He’s offices to ride the four seasons and seasonally hand down the people’s tasks.
translations.idiomatic: Wise kings revere Heaven and nurture the people, setting calendar officers to harmonize the four seasons and time the agrarian commands.

sentences.zh: 君動靜以道，奉順陰陽，則日月光明，風雨時節，寒暑調和。

translations.literal: If the lord’s movement and stillness follow the Way, honoring and following yin and yang, then sun and moon are bright, wind and rain keep their seasons, cold and heat are tuned.
translations.idiomatic: When the ruler’s every act follows the Way and yin and yang, the luminaries shine clear, rain and wind arrive on time, and cold and heat balance.

sentences.zh: 三者得敘，則災害不生，五穀熟，絲麻遂，草木茂，鳥獸蕃，民不夭疾，衣食有餘。

translations.literal: When these three obtain their proper order, then disasters and prodigies do not arise, the five grains ripen, silk and hemp succeed, grass and trees flourish, birds and beasts multiply, the people do not die young of sickness, and clothing and food are more than enough.
translations.idiomatic: When those three stay ordered, omens cease, grain and silk flourish, plants and beasts thrive, and the people live out their years in plenty.

sentences.zh: 若是，則君尊民說，上下亡怨，政教不違，禮讓可興。

translations.literal: If it is so, then the ruler is honored and the people pleased, high and low without resentment, government and instruction are not violated, and yielding courtesy may rise.
translations.idiomatic: Then the ruler is revered, the people content, high and low free of rancor, policy untainted, and courtesy can grow.

sentences.zh: 夫風雨不時，則傷農桑；

translations.literal: If wind and rain do not keep their seasons, then farming and sericulture are harmed;
translations.idiomatic: When wind and rain ignore the seasons, farming and silkworms suffer.

sentences.zh: 農桑傷，則民饑寒；

translations.literal: when farming and sericulture are harmed, then the people are cold and hungry;
translations.idiomatic: When those fail, the people face cold and hunger.

sentences.zh: 饑寒在身，則亡廉恥，寇賊奸宄所繇生也。

translations.literal: when cold and hunger are on the body, then there is no modesty and shame, and bandits, thieves, and villainy are thereby born.
translations.idiomatic: Want strips away shame and breeds banditry.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為陰陽者，王事之本，群生之命，自古賢聖未有不繇者也。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly considers that yin and yang are the root of kingly affairs and the mandate of the host of living beings; from antiquity worthies and sages have never not followed them.
translations.idiomatic: Yin and yang are the root of kingship and the breath of every creature—no sage ever ignored them.

sentences.zh: 天子之義，必純取法天地，而觀於先聖。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven’s duty must purely take law from Heaven and earth and look to former sages.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven must model himself wholly on Heaven and earth and on the ancient sages.

sentences.zh: 高皇帝所述書《天子所服第八》曰：「大謁者臣章受詔長樂宮，曰：『令群臣議天子所服，以安治天下。』

translations.literal: In what Gaodi set forth, the document ‘What the Son of Heaven Wears, Number Eight,’ says: ‘Grand usher your servant Zhang received edict at Changle Palace, saying: Order the host of ministers to deliberate what the Son of Heaven shall wear, thereby to pacify and order all under Heaven.’
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu’s compiled ordinances include ‘Imperial Dress, Section Eight’: Grand Usher Zhang announced at Changle Palace an edict bidding ministers settle the ruler’s seasonal robes so the realm might be governed in peace.

sentences.zh: 相國臣何、御史大夫臣昌謹與將軍臣陵、太子太傅臣通等議：『春夏秋冬天子所服，當法天地之數，中得人和。

translations.literal: Chancellor your servant He and Imperial Clerk your servant Chang respectfully with General your servant Ling, Grand Tutor to the Heir Apparent your servant Tong, and others deliberated: ‘In spring, summer, autumn, and winter what the Son of Heaven wears should model Heaven and earth’s numbers and hit the mean of human harmony.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Xiao He and Imperial Clerk Zhou Chang, with generals and tutors, resolved that the ruler’s seasonal dress must mirror Heaven’s numerology and human balance.

sentences.zh: 故自天子王侯有土之君，下及兆民，能法天地，順四時，以治國家，身亡禍殃，年壽永究，是奉宗廟安天下之大禮也。

translations.literal: Therefore from the Son of Heaven, kings, and feudal lords who possess soil, down to the myriad commoners, whoever can model Heaven and earth and follow the four seasons to order the state will personally meet no misfortune or calamity, and years and life will reach their utmost—this is the great ritual of serving the ancestral temples and pacifying all under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: From Son of Heaven to commoner, whoever aligns conduct with Heaven’s seasons will escape calamity, live out full years, and secure the shrines—this is the great rite of ordering the realm.

sentences.zh: 臣請法之。

translations.literal: Your servants beg to take them as model.
translations.idiomatic: We ask to adopt these rules.

sentences.zh: 中謁者趙堯舉春，李舜舉夏，□湯舉秋，貢禹舉冬，四人各職一時。』

translations.literal: Palace usher Zhao Yao presents spring, Li Shun presents summer, [text missing] Tang presents autumn, Gong Yu presents winter—the four men each take charge of one season.’
translations.idiomatic: Palace usher Zhao Yao was assigned spring, Li Shun summer, a certain Tang autumn, and Gong Yu winter—each officer one season.’

sentences.zh: 大謁者襄章奏，制曰：『可。』」

translations.literal: Grand usher Xiang Zhang memorialized; the edict said: ‘Approved.’
translations.idiomatic: Grand Usher Xiang Zhang laid the proposal before the throne; the edict read: ‘Approved.’

sentences.zh: 孝文皇帝時，以二月施恩惠於天下，賜孝弟力田及罷軍卒，祠死事者，頗非時節。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xiaowen’s time, in the second month he spread favor through the empire, bestowing on the filial, fraternal, and diligent farmers and on dismissed soldiers, sacrificing to those who died in service—somewhat not according to season.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen once showered favors in the second month—filial sons, diligent farmers, discharged veterans, rites for the war dead—slightly off the canonical calendar.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫朝錯時為太子家令，奏言其狀。

translations.literal: Imperial Clerk Chao Cuo, who was then heir apparent household steward, memorialized the situation.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Clerk Chao Cuo, then steward to the heir apparent, reported the irregularity.

sentences.zh: 臣相伏念陛下恩澤甚厚，然而災氣未息，竊恐詔令有未合當時者也。

translations.literal: Your servant Xiang prostrate considers that Your Majesty’s grace and moisture are very thick, yet baleful qi has not ceased; your servant secretly fears there are edicts not fitting the present season.
translations.idiomatic: Your kindness runs deep, yet ill omens persist—I fear some edicts still mistime seasonal policy.

sentences.zh: 願陛下選明經通知陰陽者四人，各主一時，時至明言所職，以和陰陽，天下幸甚！

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty select four men clear in the Classics who understand yin and yang, each to preside over one season; when the season arrives let them openly declare their duties, thereby to harmonize yin and yang—under Heaven there will be great fortune!
translations.idiomatic: Choose four classicists versed in yin and yang, charge each with a season, and let them announce proper observances when their turn comes—so may Heaven and earth harmonize.

sentences.zh: 相數陳便宜，上納用焉。

translations.literal: Xiang repeatedly set forth conveniences; the sovereign adopted and used them.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang pressed practical reforms and the emperor adopted them.

sentences.zh: 相敕掾史案事郡國及休告從家還至府，輒白四方異聞，或有逆賊風雨災變，郡不上，相輒奏言之。

translations.literal: Xiang ordered his clerks, when investigating affairs in commanderies and kingdoms or returning from leave at home to the bureau, always to report unusual news from the four quarters; if there were rebel bands, wind, rain, or prodigious change and the commandery did not report up, Xiang then memorialized and spoke of it.
translations.idiomatic: He told his staff to bring him every rumor from the provinces when they returned from inspection or leave, and if a commandery hid rebellion or disaster he reported it himself.

sentences.zh: 時，丙吉為御史大夫，同心輔政，上皆重之。

translations.literal: At the time Bing Ji was imperial clerk; with one heart they assisted government; the sovereign esteemed both.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji was then imperial censor; the two men worked in concert and the emperor honored both.

sentences.zh: 相為人嚴毅，不如吉寬。

translations.literal: Xiang as a man was stern and firm, not as lenient as Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang was sterner and less indulgent than Bing Ji.

sentences.zh: 視事九歲，神爵三年薨，謚曰憲侯。

translations.literal: After nine years in office, in the third year of Shenjue he passed away; his posthumous title was Marquis Xian.
translations.idiomatic: He served nine years and died in 59 BCE, posthumously titled Marquis Xian (‘Discerning’).

sentences.zh: 子弘嗣，甘露中有罪削爵為關內侯。

translations.literal: His son Hong succeeded; in the Ganlu period he had guilt and his noble rank was cut to marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: His son Hong inherited the title but lost it to marquis-within-the-passes rank after a conviction in the Ganlu era.

sentences.zh: 丙吉字少卿，魯國人也。

translations.literal: Bing Ji, courtesy name Shaoqing, was a man of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji, courtesy name Shaoqing, was a native of Lu.

sentences.zh: 治律令，為魯獄史。

translations.literal: He studied statutes and ordinances and became Lu’s jail clerk.
translations.idiomatic: He studied law and served as clerk of Lu’s prisons.

sentences.zh: 積功勞，稍遷至廷尉右監。

translations.literal: Accumulating merit and toil, he gradually rose to right superintendent of the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Merit promotions carried him up to right superintendent under the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 坐法失官，歸為州從事。

translations.literal: He sat under the law and lost office, returning to serve as staff to the provincial inspector.
translations.idiomatic: A legal fault cost him his post, and he went home as a provincial inspector’s aide.

sentences.zh: 武帝末，巫蠱事起，吉以故廷尉監征，詔治巫蠱郡邸獄。

translations.literal: At the end of Emperor Wu, when the witchcraft affair arose, Ji because he had formerly been superintendent of the commandant of justice was summoned, edicted to try the witchcraft case in the commandery hostel prison.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Emperor Wu’s reign, when the witchcraft trials began, Bing Ji—once a justice superintendent—was recalled to oversee the witchcraft jail in the imperial hostel.

sentences.zh: 時，宣帝生數月，以皇曾孫坐衛太子事系，吉見而憐之。

translations.literal: At the time Emperor Xuan was several months old; because of the imperial great-grandson he sat bound in the Heir Apparent Wei’s affair; Ji saw him and pitied him.
translations.idiomatic: The future Emperor Xuan was only months old when he was jailed as the great-grandson in the Crown Prince Wei case; Bing Ji saw the infant and pitied him.

sentences.zh: 又心知太子無事實，重哀曾孫無辜，吉擇謹厚女徒，令保養曾孫，置閒燥處。

translations.literal: Also in his heart he knew the heir apparent had no factual guilt; doubly he pitied the great-grandson’s innocence; Ji selected a careful and honest female convict, ordered her to nurture the great-grand grandson, and placed him in a quiet, dry place.
translations.idiomatic: Knowing the crown prince was innocent and the child blameless, he chose a trustworthy woman convict to nurse the boy and lodged him in a warm, quiet cell.

sentences.zh: 吉治巫蠱事，連歲不決。

translations.literal: Ji handled the witchcraft affair; year after year it was not decided.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji oversaw the witchcraft trials, which dragged on unresolved year after year.

sentences.zh: 後元二年，武帝疾，往來長楊、五柞宮，望氣者言長安獄中有天子氣，於是上遣使者分條中都官詔獄系者，亡輕重一切皆殺之。

translations.literal: In the second year of Houyuan, Emperor Wu was ill, going back and forth between Changyang and Wuzuo palaces; a qi-watcher said there was Son of Heaven qi in the Chang’an prisons; thereupon the sovereign dispatched envoys to itemize those bound in the capital offices’ edict prisons, without regard to light or heavy guilt, all to be killed.
translations.idiomatic: In 87 BCE, while Emperor Wu shuttled between hunting palaces, a diviner claimed Son-of-Heaven omens over the Chang’an jails; the emperor then sent agents to enumerate every prisoner in the capital’s edict jails and execute them all, whatever their offense.

sentences.zh: 內謁者令郭穰夜到郡邸獄，吉閉門拒使者不納，曰：「皇曾孫在。

translations.literal: Palace usher director Guo Rang came by night to the commandery hostel prison; Ji closed the gate and refused the envoy, not admitting him, saying: ‘The imperial great-grandson is here.
translations.idiomatic: When Guo Rang, director of palace ushers, reached the hostel prison at night, Bing Ji barred the gate and said, ‘The imperial great-grandson is inside.

sentences.zh: 他人亡辜死者猶不可，況親曾孫乎！」

translations.literal: For others to die without guilt is still not permissible—how much more the close great-grandson!’
translations.idiomatic: You cannot slaughter the innocent—least of all the emperor’s own great-grandson!’

sentences.zh: 相守至天明不得入，穰還以聞，因劾奏吉。

translations.literal: They held each other off until dawn and could not enter; Rang returned and reported it, then impeached and memorialized Ji.
translations.idiomatic: They stood off until dawn; Guo Rang reported back and memorialized against Bing Ji.

sentences.zh: 武帝亦寤，曰：「天使之也。」

translations.literal: Emperor Wu also awoke to it, saying: ‘Heaven made it so.’
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu came to his senses and said, ‘Heaven meant to spare him.’

sentences.zh: 因赦天下。

translations.literal: Thereupon he amnestied all under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: He then proclaimed a general amnesty.

sentences.zh: 郡邸獄系者獨賴吉得生，恩及四海矣。

translations.literal: Those bound in the commandery hostel prison alone relied on Ji to obtain life—grace reached the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: Only the inmates of that hostel prison lived because of Bing Ji—a kindness that spread across the realm.

sentences.zh: 曾孫病，幾不全者數焉，吉數敕保養乳母加致醫藥，視遇甚有恩惠，以私財物給其衣食。

translations.literal: The great-grandson fell ill; several times he nearly did not survive; Ji repeatedly ordered the nurse to add physicians and medicines, treated him with very much kindness and grace, and supplied his clothing and food from private property.
translations.idiomatic: The boy nearly died several times; Bing Ji ordered better care, summoned doctors, and paid for food and clothes from his own purse.

sentences.zh: 後吉為車騎將軍軍市令，遷大將軍長史，霍光甚重之，入為光祿大夫給事中。

translations.literal: Later Ji became market director for the chariot-and-cavalry general’s army, was promoted grand general’s chief clerk; Huo Guang greatly esteemed him, and he entered as supernumerary household grandee with palace access.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to army market director under the chariot-and-cavalry general, then chief clerk to the grand general; Huo Guang valued him highly and brought him into court as supernumerary household grandee.

sentences.zh: 昭帝崩，無嗣，大將軍光遣吉迎昌邑王賀。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhao passed away without heir; Grand General Guang sent Ji to welcome King He of Changyi.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhao died heirless, Huo Guang sent Bing Ji to escort Prince He of Changyi to the capital.

sentences.zh: 賀即位，以行淫亂廢，光與車騎將軍張安世諸大臣議所立，未定。

translations.literal: When He assumed the throne, because of conduct of debauchery and disorder he was deposed; Guang with Chariot-and-Cavalry General Zhang Anshi and the various great ministers deliberated whom to establish, not yet settled.
translations.idiomatic: Prince He was enthroned but deposed for debauchery; Huo Guang and Zhang Anshi debated a replacement without reaching agreement.

sentences.zh: 吉奏記光曰：「將軍事孝武皇帝，受襁褓之屬，任天下之寄，孝昭皇帝早崩亡嗣，海內憂懼，欲亟聞嗣主，發喪之日以大誼立後，所立非其人，復以大誼廢之，天下莫不服焉。

translations.literal: Ji submitted a sealed note to Guang saying: ‘General, you served Emperor Wu, received the swaddling-clothes charge, bore the empire’s trust; Emperor Zhao passed early without heir; within the seas there was worry and fear, eager quickly to hear of a successor lord; on the day mourning was announced you by great duty established a successor; the one established was not the right man, again by great duty you deposed him—none under Heaven failed to submit.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji wrote Huo Guang: you served Emperor Wu, accepted the regency for the infant emperor, and hold the realm’s fate; when Zhao died without heir the empire panicked; you enthroned a successor on the day of mourning, then deposed him when he proved unworthy—everyone accepted both acts as righteous.

sentences.zh: 方今社稷宗廟群生之命在將軍之一舉。

translations.literal: At present the altars of soil and grain, the ancestral temples, and the host of living beings’ fate hang on a single move of the general’s.
translations.idiomatic: Now the fate of shrines, altars, and every living soul turns on your next choice.

sentences.zh: 竊伏聽於眾庶，察其所言，諸侯宗室在位列者，未有所聞於民間也。

translations.literal: Your servant has prostrated himself to listen among the multitude and observe what they say; among the feudal lords and imperial lineage who hold rank, none has been heard of favorably among the common folk.
translations.idiomatic: I have listened to common talk: none of the ranked princes or imperial kinsmen wins the people’s hope.

sentences.zh: 而遺詔所養武帝曾孫名病已在掖庭外家者，吉前使居郡邸時見其幼少，至今十八九矣，通經術，有美材，行安而節和。

translations.literal: Yet the testamentary edict’s nurtured imperial great-grandson named Bingyi, dwelling with his maternal kin outside the Lateral Courts—Ji earlier, when he had him stay at the commandery hostel, saw him in childhood; to the present he is eighteen or nineteen, understands the classics and techniques, has fine talent, conduct steady and temper harmonious.
translations.idiomatic: The edict-nurtured great-grandson Liu Bingyi, raised outside the harem—whom Bing Ji had known as a child in the hostel—is now a young man of eighteen or nineteen, learned, talented, and even-tempered.

sentences.zh: 願將軍詳大議，參以蓍龜，豈宜褒顯，先使入侍，令天下昭然知之，然後決定大策，天下幸甚！」

translations.literal: I beg the general to examine the great deliberation, consult tortoise and milfoil—how could it be unsuitable to honor and display him? First have him enter attendance; let all under Heaven plainly know it—then decide the great plan; under Heaven will be greatly fortunate!’
translations.idiomatic: Consult the great portents and the oracles, bring him forward as heir apparent so the realm may know your intent, then settle the succession—such would be Heaven’s blessing!’

sentences.zh: 光覽其議，遂尊立皇曾孫，遣宗正劉德與吉迎曾孫於掖庭。

translations.literal: Guang read his proposal, then honored and established the imperial great-grandson, dispatching Director of Imperial Clan Liu De together with Ji to welcome the great-grandson from the Lateral Courts.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang accepted the plan, elevated Liu Bingyi, and sent Liu De with Bing Ji to fetch him from the harem compound.

sentences.zh: 宣帝初即位，賜吉爵關內侯。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xuan first assumed the throne, he bestowed on Ji noble rank as marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan ennobled Bing Ji as a marquis-within-the-passes as soon as he took the throne.

sentences.zh: 吉為人深厚，不伐善。

translations.literal: Ji as a man was deep and thick, not boasting of good deeds.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji was reserved and deep, never boasting of his kindness.

sentences.zh: 自曾孫遭遇，吉絕口不道前恩，故朝廷莫能明其功也。

translations.literal: From the great-grandson’s good fortune, Ji sealed his lips and did not speak of prior grace; therefore the court could not clarify his merit.
translations.idiomatic: After the boy became emperor, Bing Ji never mentioned his old service, so the court never knew his full merit.

sentences.zh: 地節三年，立皇太子，吉為太子太傅，數月，遷御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the third year of Dijie, the imperial heir was established; Ji became grand tutor to the heir; after several months he was promoted imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: In 67 BCE he was made grand tutor to the new crown prince and within months promoted to imperial censor.

sentences.zh: 及霍氏誅，上躬親政，省尚書事。

translations.literal: When the Huo clan was executed, the sovereign personally handled government and reduced the Secretariat’s business.
translations.idiomatic: After the Huo family purge the emperor took personal charge and trimmed the inner court’s paperwork.

sentences.zh: 是時，掖庭宮婢則令民夫上書，自陳嘗有阿保之功。

translations.literal: At this time a Lateral Courts palace maid named Ze had a commoner husband submit a memorial, declaring she had once had wet-nurse merit.
translations.idiomatic: Then a harem woman named Ze had her husband petition, claiming she had nursed the emperor as an infant.

sentences.zh: 章下掖庭令考問，則辭引使者丙吉知狀。

translations.literal: The document was sent down to the Lateral Courts director for examination; Ze’s testimony cited envoy Bing Ji as knowing the facts.
translations.idiomatic: The case went to the harem director; Ze named Bing Ji as witness.

sentences.zh: 掖庭令將則詣御史府以視吉。

translations.literal: The Lateral Courts director led Ze to the censor’s bureau to show Ji.
translations.idiomatic: The director brought Ze to the censor’s office to confront Bing Ji.

sentences.zh: 吉識，謂則曰：「汝嘗坐養皇曾孫不謹督笞，汝安得有功？

translations.literal: Ji recognized her and said to Ze: ‘You once sat for nursing the imperial great-grandson negligently and were whipped—how could you have merit?
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji recognized her: ‘You were flogged for negligent nursing—what merit is that?

sentences.zh: 獨渭城胡組、淮陽郭徵卿有恩耳。」

translations.literal: Only Weicheng’s Hu Zu and Huaiyang’s Guo Zhengqing had grace.’
translations.idiomatic: Only Hu Zu of Weicheng and Guo Zhengqing of Huaiyang truly helped.’

sentences.zh: 分別奏組等共養勞苦狀。

translations.literal: He separately memorialized the form of Zu and others’ joint nurturing and toil.
translations.idiomatic: He filed a separate memorial on Hu Zu and Guo Zhengqing’s care.

sentences.zh: 詔吉求組、征卿，已死，有子孫，皆受厚賞。

translations.literal: An edict ordered Ji to seek Zu and Zhengqing; they were already dead; where there were sons and grandsons, all received thick rewards.
translations.idiomatic: The court sought Hu Zu and Guo Zhengqing; both were dead, but their descendants were richly rewarded.

sentences.zh: 詔免則為庶人，賜錢十萬。

translations.literal: An edict pardoned Ze to commoner status and bestowed one hundred thousand cash.
translations.idiomatic: Ze was stripped to commoner rank but given a hundred thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 上親見問，然後知吉有舊恩，而終不言。

translations.literal: The sovereign personally saw and questioned him, then knew Ji had old grace yet to the end did not speak.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor questioned her himself and learned of Bing Ji’s silence about his old debt.

sentences.zh: 上大賢之，制詔丞相：「朕微眇時，御史大夫吉與朕有舊恩，厥德茂焉。

translations.literal: The sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and edicted the chancellor: ‘In Our mean and slight time, Imperial Clerk Ji had old grace toward Us; his virtue is luxuriant.
translations.idiomatic: Deeply moved, the emperor told the chancellor: ‘When I was helpless, Censor Bing Ji saved me; his virtue is profound.

sentences.zh: 《詩》不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does not the Odes say?
translations.idiomatic: Does not the Odes say?

sentences.zh: 『亡德不報』。

translations.literal: ‘No virtue goes unrequited.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘No good deed goes unrewarded.’

sentences.zh: 其封吉為博陽侯，邑千三百戶。」

translations.literal: Enfeoff Ji as marquis of Boyang, with a fief of one thousand three hundred households.’
translations.idiomatic: Enfeoff him as Marquis of Boyang with thirteen hundred households.’

sentences.zh: 臨當封，吉疾病，上將使人加紳而封之，及其生存也。

translations.literal: As the enfeoffment was about to occur, Ji fell ill; the sovereign was about to send someone to add the sash and enfeoff him while he still lived.
translations.idiomatic: When the patent was ready, Bing Ji fell ill; the emperor meant to invest him in person while he still lived.

sentences.zh: 上憂吉疾不起，太子太傅夏侯勝曰：「此未死也。

translations.literal: The sovereign worried Ji’s illness would not rise; Grand Tutor to the Heir Apparent Xiahou Sheng said: ‘This is not yet death.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing Bing Ji might die, the emperor heard Grand Tutor Xiahou Sheng say, ‘He is not dying yet.

sentences.zh: 臣聞有陰德者，必饗其樂以及子孫。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that those with hidden virtue are sure to enjoy their blessing reaching sons and grandsons.
translations.idiomatic: Men of hidden virtue enjoy blessings down to their descendants.

sentences.zh: 今吉未獲報而疾甚，非其死疾也。」

translations.literal: Now Ji has not yet received recompense while his illness is severe—this is not the illness of his death.’
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji has not yet been repaid; this illness will not kill him.’

sentences.zh: 後病果愈。

translations.literal: Afterward his illness indeed recovered.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji indeed recovered.

sentences.zh: 吉上書固辭，自陳不宜以空名受賞。

translations.literal: Ji submitted a memorial firmly declining, declaring himself unsuited to receive reward on an empty name.
translations.idiomatic: He memorialized to decline, saying he deserved no empty honor.

sentences.zh: 上報曰：「朕之封君，非空名也，而君上書歸侯印，是顯朕不德也。

translations.literal: The sovereign replied: ‘Our enfeoffing you, lord, is not an empty name; yet you, lord, submit a memorial returning the marquis seal—this displays Our lack of virtue.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered: ‘This title is no empty gesture; returning the seal would only shame me.

sentences.zh: 方今天下少事，君其專精神，省思慮，近醫藥，以自持。」

translations.literal: At present under Heaven there are few affairs; you, lord, should concentrate your spirit, reduce anxious thought, draw near to physicians and medicine, thereby to sustain yourself.’
translations.idiomatic: Times are quiet; gather your strength, ease your mind, and follow the doctors’ care.’

sentences.zh: 後五歲，代魏相為丞相。

translations.literal: After five years he replaced Wei Xiang as chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Five years later he succeeded Wei Xiang as chancellor.

sentences.zh: 吉本起獄法小吏，後學《詩》、《禮》，皆通大義。

translations.literal: Ji originally rose from petty clerks in prisons and law; afterward he studied the Odes and Rites, both penetrating their great meaning.
translations.idiomatic: He had begun as a jail clerk, then mastered the Odes and Rites in their larger sense.

sentences.zh: 及居相位，上寬大，好禮讓。

translations.literal: When he occupied the chancellor’s seat, he was magnanimous and fond of yielding courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: As chancellor he was broad-minded and promoted courtesy.

sentences.zh: 掾史有罪臧，不稱職，輒予長休告，終無所案驗。

translations.literal: When clerks had guilt or bribery or were not fit for office, he always gave them long leave to rest and report, and in the end never investigated or verified.
translations.idiomatic: Clerks who took bribes or failed at duty were simply given extended leave rather than prosecuted.

sentences.zh: 客或謂吉曰：「君侯為漢相，奸吏成其私，然無所懲艾。」

translations.literal: A client once said to Ji: ‘You, lord marquis, are Han’s chancellor; wicked clerks complete their private aims, yet there is no punishment.’
translations.idiomatic: A retainer protested that corrupt clerks went unpunished under Han’s chief minister.

sentences.zh: 吉曰：「夫以三公之府有案吏之名，吾竊陋焉。」

translations.literal: Ji said: ‘That the three dukes’ bureau should have the name of investigating clerks—your servant privately finds it mean.’
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji replied, ‘I would be ashamed to have the chancellor’s office known chiefly for prosecuting clerks.’

sentences.zh: 後人代吉，因以為故事，公府不案吏，自吉始。

translations.literal: Later men replaced Ji and took it as precedent—the ducal bureau does not investigate clerks; it began from Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Later chancellors followed his precedent: the high ministry no longer hounded minor officials—that began with Bing Ji.

sentences.zh: 於官屬掾史，務掩過揚善。

translations.literal: Toward official subordinates and clerks, he strove to cover faults and display good.
translations.idiomatic: With subordinates he preferred to hide faults and praise strengths.

sentences.zh: 吉馭吏耆酒，數逋蕩，嘗從吉出，醉嘔丞相車上。

translations.literal: Ji’s carriage driver loved ale, often defaulted and roamed; once when following Ji out, drunk he vomited on the chancellor’s carriage.
translations.idiomatic: His coachman was a drunk who once vomited on the chancellor’s cushions.

sentences.zh: 西曹主吏白欲斥之，吉曰：「以醉飽之失去士，使此人將復何所容？

translations.literal: The western bureau chief clerk reported wishing to dismiss him; Ji said: ‘Because of losing a gentleman to drunken satiety, if this man is driven off, where will he again find a place?
translations.idiomatic: The chief clerk wanted him dismissed; Bing Ji said, ‘For a full belly’s slip I will not ruin a man—where could he find another post?

sentences.zh: 西曹地忍之，此不過污丞相車茵耳。」

translations.literal: Western bureau, bear with it—this is no more than soiling the chancellor’s carriage cushion.’
translations.idiomatic: Let it go—it is only a stained seat.’

sentences.zh: 遂不去也。

translations.literal: Thereupon he was not dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: The coachman kept his job.

sentences.zh: 此馭吏邊郡人，習知邊塞發奔命警備事，嘗出，適見驛騎持赤白囊，邊郡發奔命書馳來至。

translations.literal: This carriage driver was a border commandery man, practiced in knowing border garrison urgent-call and alert matters; once when going out he happened to see post riders bearing red and white pouches—border commanderies had dispatched urgent-call documents galloping to arrive.
translations.idiomatic: The man knew frontier signals; one day he saw couriers with red-and-white pouches galloping in with emergency dispatches from the border.

sentences.zh: 馭吏因隨驛騎至公車刺取，知虜入雲中、代郡，遽歸府見吉白狀，因曰：「恐虜所入邊郡，二千石長吏有老病不任兵馬者，宜可豫視。」

translations.literal: The carriage driver therefore followed the post rider to the public carriage office and spied, learning the barbarians had entered Yunzhong and Dai commanderies; he hurried back to the bureau, saw Ji, and reported the situation, then said: ‘I fear in the border commanderies the barbarians entered, among two-thousand-piculs senior clerks there are old and sick who cannot bear arms and horses—it is fitting beforehand to review.’
translations.idiomatic: He trailed them to the courier office, learned the Xiongnu had struck Yunzhong and Dai, rushed back, and urged Bing Ji to review which frontier governors were too old or ill to command defense.

sentences.zh: 吉善其言，召東曹案邊長吏，瑣科條其人。

translations.literal: Ji thought well of his words, summoned the eastern bureau to review border senior clerks, itemizing those persons by petty regulations.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji agreed, ordered his staff to list border governors’ fitness for command.

sentences.zh: 未已，詔召丞相、御史，問以虜所入郡吏，吉具對。

translations.literal: Before it was finished, an edict summoned chancellor and imperial clerk, asking about the clerks of the commanderies the barbarians entered; Ji answered in full.
translations.idiomatic: Before they finished, the palace summoned chancellor and censor to name the frontier officials; Bing Ji answered from his lists.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫卒遽不能詳知，以得譴讓。

translations.literal: The imperial clerk in the end was hasty and could not know in detail, thereby incurring rebuke and blame.
translations.idiomatic: The imperial censor, caught unprepared, was scolded for ignorance.

sentences.zh: 而吉見謂憂邊思職，馭吏力也。

translations.literal: While Ji was said to worry over the border and think on duty—it was the carriage driver’s force.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji passed for a border-minded chancellor thanks to his driver.

sentences.zh: 吉乃歎曰：「士亡不可容，能各有所長。

translations.literal: Ji then sighed, saying: ‘Officers are none not tolerable; each can have his special strength.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji sighed, ‘Every man has his use.

sentences.zh: 向使丞相不先聞馭吏言，何見勞勉之有？」

translations.literal: Had the chancellor not first heard the carriage driver’s words, how could there have been this labor and encouragement?’
translations.idiomatic: Had I not heard my driver, how would I have earned such praise?’

sentences.zh: 掾史繇是益賢吉。

translations.literal: Thereupon the clerks all the more deemed Ji worthy.
translations.idiomatic: His staff admired him the more for seeing the larger picture.

sentences.zh: 吉又嘗出，逢清道群鬥者，死傷橫道，吉過之不問，掾史獨怪之。

translations.literal: Ji again once went out, met a road-clearing crowd brawling; dead and wounded lay across the road; Ji passed by without asking—the clerks alone thought it strange.
translations.idiomatic: Once he passed a street fight and corpses without stopping; his clerks thought it odd.

sentences.zh: 吉前行，逢人逐牛，牛喘吐舌，吉止駐，使騎吏問：「逐牛行幾里矣？」

translations.literal: Ji went forward; he met a man driving an ox; the ox panted and stuck out its tongue; Ji halted his carriage and sent a mounted clerk to ask: ‘How many li has the ox been driven?’
translations.idiomatic: Farther on he stopped to ask a farmer how far his winded ox had been driven.

sentences.zh: 掾史獨謂丞相前後失問，或以譏吉，吉曰：「民斗相殺傷，長安令、京兆尹職所當禁備逐捕，歲竟丞相課其殿最，奏行賞罰而已。

translations.literal: The clerks alone thought the chancellor had failed to ask front and back; some used it to ridicule Ji; Ji said: ‘When commoners fight and kill or wound one another, the Chang’an magistrate and Governor of the Capital’s duty is what ought to forbid, prepare, pursue, and capture; at year’s end the chancellor grades their low and high and memorializes to carry out rewards and punishments—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: His men mocked him for caring more about an ox than murder; Bing Ji said street brawls are the magistrate’s business while the chancellor only grades officials at year’s end.

sentences.zh: 宰相不親小事，非所當於道路問也。

translations.literal: The chancellor does not personally handle small matters; it is not what ought to be asked on the road.
translations.idiomatic: A chancellor does not meddle in such trifles on the highway.

sentences.zh: 方春少陽用事，未可大熱，恐牛近行，用暑故喘，此時氣失節，恐有所傷害也。

translations.literal: Just now spring, minor yang is in charge; it ought not yet be greatly hot; I fear the ox has traveled near, and because of heat therefore pants—at this time the qi has missed its season; I fear there is harm and injury.
translations.idiomatic: In early spring an ox should not pant from heat; if it does, the seasons are wrong—a matter that threatens the harvest.

sentences.zh: 三公典調和陰陽，職當憂，是以問之。」

translations.literal: The three dukes take charge of harmonizing yin and yang; the duty is fitting to worry—therefore I asked it.’
translations.idiomatic: The three high ministers must watch yin and yang; that is why I asked.’

sentences.zh: 掾史乃服，以吉知大體。

translations.literal: The clerks then submitted, deeming Ji to know the great frame.
translations.idiomatic: The staff then conceded that he grasped the chancellor’s true scope.

sentences.zh: 元帝時，長安士伍尊上書言：「臣少時為郡邸小吏，竊見孝宣皇帝以皇曾孫在郡邸獄。

translations.literal: In Emperor Yuan’s time, Chang’an commoner Zun submitted a memorial saying: ‘Your servant in youth was a petty clerk in the commandery hostel and secretly saw Emperor Xuan as imperial great-grandson in the commandery hostel prison.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Yuan, a Chang’an commoner named Zun memorialized that as a young hostel clerk he had seen the future Emperor Xuan jailed there.

sentences.zh: 是時，治獄使者丙吉見皇曾孫遭離無辜，吉仁心感動，涕泣淒惻，選擇復作胡組養視皇孫，吉常從。

translations.literal: At that time, trial-prison envoy Bing Ji saw the imperial great-grandson suffer separation and innocence; Ji’s humane heart was moved, weeping in bitter sorrow; he selected a female convict on revolving labor, Hu Zu, to nurture and watch the imperial grandson; Ji constantly accompanied.
translations.idiomatic: Envoy Bing Ji, moved by the child’s innocence, wept and assigned Hu Zu, a woman on labor rotation, to nurse him while he stayed at hand.

sentences.zh: 臣尊日再侍臥庭上。

translations.literal: Your servant Zun twice daily attended, lying in the courtyard.
translations.idiomatic: I, Zun, slept twice daily in the courtyard beside him.

sentences.zh: 後遭條獄之召，吉扞拒大難，不避嚴刑峻法。

translations.literal: Later he encountered the summons of the itemized prisons; Ji resisted the great difficulty, not avoiding harsh law.
translations.idiomatic: When the mass execution order came, Bing Ji defied it at risk to himself.

sentences.zh: 既遭大赦，吉謂守丞誰知，皇孫不當在官，使誰如移書京兆尹，遣與胡組俱送京兆尹，不受，復還。

translations.literal: After encountering the great amnesty, Ji told assistant warden She Zhi that the imperial grandson ought not remain in official custody; he ordered She Ru to forward documents to the Governor of the Capital and sent him together with Hu Zu to deliver the boy to the Governor of the Capital; the Governor of the Capital would not accept him, and they returned.
translations.idiomatic: After the general amnesty Bing Ji told assistant warden She Zhi that the imperial grandson could not stay in jail; he sent She Ru with transfer papers to the Governor of the Capital and had Hu Zu accompany them, but the governor refused to take the child and they had to return.

sentences.zh: 及組日滿當去，皇孫思慕，吉以私錢顧組，令留與郭徽卿並養數月，乃遣組去。

translations.literal: When Zu’s term was full and she ought to depart, the imperial grandson longed for her; Ji with private cash hired Zu, ordering her to remain and together with Guo Zhengqing jointly nurture several months, then sent Zu off.
translations.idiomatic: When Hu Zu’s term ended, Bing Ji paid her from his own pocket to stay on with Guo Zhengqing a few months longer.

sentences.zh: 後少內嗇夫白吉曰：『食皇孫亡詔令』。

translations.literal: Later the minor inner granary scribe reported to Ji saying: ‘Feeding the imperial grandson—there is no edict order.’
translations.idiomatic: The inner granary clerk then said there was no edict to feed the boy.

sentences.zh: 時，吉得食米肉，月月以給皇孫。

translations.literal: At the time Ji obtained food, rice, and meat, month by month to supply the imperial grandson.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji supplied rice and meat each month from his own rations.

sentences.zh: 吉即時病，輒使臣尊朝夕請問皇孫，視省席蓐燥濕。

translations.literal: Ji immediately fell ill; then he ordered your servant Zun morning and evening to inquire after the imperial grandson, inspect matting for dry and damp.
translations.idiomatic: When Bing Ji fell ill he sent me morning and night to check on the child’s bedding.

sentences.zh: 候伺組、徽卿，不得令晨夜去皇孫敖蕩，數奏甘毳食物。

translations.literal: He watched Hu Zu and Guo Zhengqing, not letting them leave the imperial grandson to roam wild morning and night, repeatedly presenting sweet and soft foods.
translations.idiomatic: He watched the nurses so they would not neglect the boy and sent delicate food.

sentences.zh: 所以擁全神靈，成育聖躬，功德已無量矣。

translations.literal: By these means he embraced and completed the numinous spirits, accomplished and nurtured the sagely person—merit and virtue were already beyond measure.
translations.idiomatic: Thus he preserved the child’s life and raised a sage—merit beyond reckoning.

sentences.zh: 時豈豫知天下之福，而徼其報哉！

translations.literal: At the time how could he have foreknown the empire’s blessing and angle for its reward!
translations.idiomatic: He could not have foreseen the throne as his reward.

sentences.zh: 誠其仁恩內結於心也。

translations.literal: Truly his humane grace knotted within the heart.
translations.idiomatic: His kindness came from the heart alone.

sentences.zh: 雖介之推割肌以存君，不足以比。

translations.literal: Though Jie Zitui sliced his flesh to preserve his lord, it is not enough to compare.
translations.idiomatic: Even Jie Zitui’s flesh-offering would not match it.

sentences.zh: 教宣皇帝時，臣上書言狀，幸得下吉，吉謙讓不敢自伐，刪去臣辭，專歸美於組、徽卿。

translations.literal: When he taught Emperor Xuan, your servant submitted a memorial stating the facts, fortunately obtaining audience below Ji; Ji modestly declined and dared not boast of himself, deleting your servant’s wording, solely attributing the praise to Zu and Zhengqing.
translations.idiomatic: When Xuan ascended I, Zun, reported the story; Bing Ji struck my lines and gave the credit to Hu Zu and Guo Zhengqing.

sentences.zh: 組、徽卿皆以受田宅賜錢，吉封為博陽侯，臣尊不得比組、徽卿。

translations.literal: Zu and Zhengqing both thereby received fields, houses, and gifted cash; Ji was enfeoffed marquis of Boyang—your servant Zun cannot compare with Zu and Zhengqing.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Zu and Guo Zhengqing received land and cash while Bing Ji was ennobled; I, Zun, cannot rank with them.

sentences.zh: 臣年老居貧，死在旦暮，欲終不言，恐使有功不著。

translations.literal: Your servant is old and dwells in poverty, death at dawn or dusk; wishing finally not to speak, yet fearing to make meritorious deeds not manifest.
translations.idiomatic: I am old and poor and near death; I would have stayed silent but feared true merit would stay hidden.

sentences.zh: 吉子顯坐微文奪爵為關內侯，臣愚以為宜復其爵邑，以報先入功德。」

translations.literal: Ji’s son Xian sat because of minute wording losing noble rank to marquis within the passes; your servant foolishly considers it fitting to restore his noble rank and fief, to repay the prior men’s merit and virtue.’
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji’s son Xian lost his marquisate on a petty charge; I ask that his title be restored to reward his father’s virtue.’

sentences.zh: 先是，顯為太僕十餘年，與官屬大為奸利，臧千餘萬，司隸校尉昌案劾，罪至不道，奏請逮捕。

translations.literal: Earlier, Xian had been grand coachman more than ten years, with office subordinates greatly practicing wicked profit, embezzling more than a thousand myriad; metropolitan commandant Chang investigated and impeached, guilt reaching lack of the Way, memorializing to request arrest.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Bing Xian, as grand coachman for over a decade, had embezzled tens of millions until Commandant Chang impeached him for inhuman crimes and sought his arrest.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「故丞相吉有舊恩，朕不忍絕。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: ‘The former chancellor Ji had old grace toward Us; We cannot bear to cut him off.’
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, ‘The late Chancellor Bing Ji served me; I cannot destroy his line.’

sentences.zh: 免顯官，奪邑四百戶。

translations.literal: He dismissed Xian from office and stripped four hundred households of the fief.
translations.idiomatic: He removed Bing Xian from office and cut four hundred households from his fief.

sentences.zh: 後復以為城門校尉。

translations.literal: Later again he made him colonel of the gates.
translations.idiomatic: Later he reappointed him colonel of the gates.

sentences.zh: 顯卒，子昌嗣爵關內侯。

translations.literal: Xian died; his son Chang inherited noble rank as marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Xian died; his son Chang inherited marquis-within-the-passes rank.

sentences.zh: 成帝時，修廢功，以吉舊恩尤重，鴻嘉元年制詔丞相御史：「蓋聞褒功德，繼絕統，所以重宗廟，廣賢聖之路也。

translations.literal: In Emperor Cheng’s time, repairing neglected merit, because Ji’s old grace was especially weighty, in the first year of Hongjia an edict ordered chancellor and imperial clerk: ‘We have heard that praising merit and virtue, continuing cut-off lines, is the means to stress the ancestral temples and broaden the road of worthies and sages.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Cheng, in 20 BCE, an edict recalled Bing Ji’s great debt: honoring merit continues broken lines and strengthens the ancestral cult.

sentences.zh: 故博陽侯吉以舊恩有功而封，今其祀絕，朕甚憐之。

translations.literal: Therefore Marquis of Boyang Ji because of old grace and merit was enfeoffed; now his sacrifices are cut off; We greatly pity it.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji was enfeoffed for old service, yet his line had failed; the emperor pitied it.

sentences.zh: 夫善善及子孫，古今之通誼也，其封吉孫中郎將、關內侯昌為博陽侯，奉吉後。」

translations.literal: Doing good to the good reaches sons and grandsons—it is the common duty of ancient and modern; enfeoff Ji’s grandson, gentleman of the palace and marquis within the passes Chang, as marquis of Boyang, to serve Ji’s posterity.’
translations.idiomatic: Good done to the good should reach descendants; he therefore enfeoffed Bing Ji’s grandson Chang, a palace gentleman and marquis-within-the-passes, as Marquis of Boyang to continue the line.’

sentences.zh: 國絕三十二歲復續雲。

translations.literal: The state was cut off thirty-two years, then continued again, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: The marquisate had lapsed for thirty-two years before the line was renewed.

sentences.zh: 昌傳子至孫，王莽時乃絕。

translations.literal: Chang passed to a son down to a grandson; in Wang Mang’s time then it was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Chang passed the title to his son and grandson until Wang Mang’s usurpation ended the line.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：古之制名，必繇象類，遠取諸物，近取諸身。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: In antiquity in instituting names, they necessarily followed images and categories, taking distantly from things, taking near at hand from the person.
translations.idiomatic: The historian’s appraisal: the ancients coined titles by analogy—drawing images from nature and from the human body.

sentences.zh: 故經謂君為元首，臣為股肱，明其一體，相待而成也。

translations.literal: Therefore the classics call the ruler the head’s origin, ministers thigh and forearm, clarifying they are one body, relying on each other to complete.
translations.idiomatic: The classics call the ruler the head and ministers the arms and legs, showing that ruler and minister form one body and complete each other.

sentences.zh: 是故君臣相配，古今常道，自然之勢也。

translations.literal: Therefore ruler and minister match one another—ancient and modern constant way, the tendency of nature.
translations.idiomatic: Their partnership is the enduring law of government, as natural as the pairing of head and limbs.

sentences.zh: 近觀漢相，高祖開基，蕭、曹為冠，孝宣中興，丙、魏有聲。

translations.literal: Looking near at Han’s chancellors: Gaozu opened the foundation, Xiao and Cao were the cap; Emperor Xiaoxuan’s mid-restoration, Bing and Wei had reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Among Han chancellors, Xiao He and Cao Can stood first at the founding; under Emperor Xuan’s restoration Bing Ji and Wei Xiang won lasting fame.

sentences.zh: 是時，黜陟有序，眾職修理，公卿多稱其位，海內興於禮讓。

translations.literal: At that time, demotions and promotions had order; the host of duties were put in good repair; dukes and ministers mostly matched their posts; within the seas yielding courtesy arose.
translations.idiomatic: In that age promotions and dismissals followed rule, every office functioned, high ministers fitted their posts, and the realm turned to courtesy and deference.

sentences.zh: 覽其行事，豈虛乎哉！

translations.literal: Viewing their deeds and conduct—how could they be empty!
translations.idiomatic: Their deeds prove that reputation was no empty praise.
